insight: kiro.cmd on Windows is the IDE launcher, NOT a CLI tool. Running 'kiro' opens the desktop IDE. Get-Command finding it with exit code 0 from --version is misleading — always verify what a command actually does before confirming it's a CLI.
Kiro IDE and Kiro CLI are separate products. The CLI is called 'kiro-cli' and as of March 2026 only supports macOS and Linux. No native Windows support — WSL is the workaround.
This user (mohdryad) has kiro-cli installed in WSL at /home/mohdryad/.local/bin/kiro-cli. Can be invoked from Windows via 'wsl -- kiro-cli' or directly from a WSL terminal.

When a user asks 'do I have X tool' on Windows, also check WSL if the tool doesn't have native Windows support. Use 'wsl -- which <tool>' or 'wsl -- bash -ic "command -v <tool>"' as fallback. The simple 'which' may fail — broader search including ~/.local/bin is more reliable.
When webFetch returns only nav/boilerplate from JS-heavy sites, retry with mode='rendered'. First attempt on kiro.dev returned empty content; rendered mode returned full docs.
For Windows users asking about CLI tools, check platform support early by fetching installation docs rather than assuming cross-platform availability. Saves a round-trip of wrong answers.

Project uses ESLint 9 flat config (eslint.config.mjs) with next/core-web-vitals and next/typescript. Custom rules: no-console warn, no-unused-vars error, no-explicit-any error. Legacy .eslintrc.json also exists but is dead weight — ESLint 9 ignores it.

When auditing linting setup, check for conflicting config formats first. ESLint 9 uses flat config by default — presence of both .eslintrc.json and eslint.config.mjs means the legacy file is ignored and should be deleted to avoid confusion.

Phantom config file pattern: .prettierrc exists but prettier is not in package.json dependencies and no format script exists. Config only works if someone has the VS Code extension — creates inconsistent formatting across team members.

Project Prettier settings (.prettierrc): semi: true, singleQuote: true, tabWidth: 2, trailingComma: es5, printWidth: 100. These need prettier + eslint-config-prettier installed to actually function in CI/scripts.

next/core-web-vitals + next/typescript presets already cover: react-hooks/rules-of-hooks, exhaustive-deps, jsx-key, no-unescaped-entities, no-img-element, no-html-link-for-pages, no-sync-scripts, font/image optimization. No need to re-declare these.

Recommended additional ESLint rules for this project's stack (React 19, Next 16, TS, Zod, react-hook-form): react/no-array-index-as-key, react/jsx-no-target-blank, react/self-closing-comp, @typescript-eslint/consistent-type-imports, react/no-unstable-nested-components.
react/react-in-jsx-scope is NOT needed — React 19 with Next.js auto-imports React, and the Next.js preset already disables this rule.

Kiro Autopilot vs Supervised mode: Autopilot makes all file changes autonomously without approval prompts. Supervised mode lets users review/revert changes. Toggle is in the chat panel UI. User asked how to pre-approve everything — answer is simply switch to Autopilot mode.

This project's section componentization workflow: (1) pick task from backlog with label 'section-componentization', (2) read EXISTING page JSX — never Webflow HTML, (3) extract verbatim into src/components/sections/, (4) replace inline block with component import, (5) export from barrel, (6) verify page is shorter, (7) mark Done. Source of truth is always the current page file, not Webflow.

src/components/sections/ barrel (index.ts) already existed with 3 exports (WhySection, CTASection, SupportMissionSection) before TASK-099 was started. Always check actual filesystem state before assuming a scaffolding task needs work — it may already be done.

PageIntroSection pattern: About, Contact, School Plan, and Curriculum pages all had identical inline JSX — Section+Container+FadeIn+div with h2+p. Careers had the same visual pattern but its intro was nested inside a shared wrapper with why_join and openings, making it non-extractable without restructuring. Always verify the structural context before assuming a pattern is extractable.

Task TASK-103 claimed 6 pages had the intro pattern (including Admissions and Careers). Admissions had no standalone intro section at all — it goes straight to a banner image. Careers intro was embedded in a shared section wrapper. Always read the actual page files before trusting the task description's page count.

When extracting a repeated JSX pattern across multiple pages, read all target page files in parallel (single readCode call per file, all simultaneous) before writing any code. This reveals structural differences (like Careers' nested intro) that would otherwise cause incorrect extractions.

PageIntroSection props interface: { id?: string; headline: string; body: string | string[]; className?: string }. The id prop was added beyond the spec to preserve existing DOM IDs used by Playwright tests. Always check if existing section IDs are referenced in tests before dropping them.

After extracting a section component, check whether Section/Container/FadeIn imports are still used elsewhere in the page file before removing them. In this project all 4 updated pages still used those primitives for other sections — no import cleanup needed.

Backlog.md task status flow for this project: To Do → In Progress (set before starting work) → Done (set after zero TS errors confirmed). Always set In Progress before touching files, Done only after getDiagnostics confirms zero errors.

For tasks that are already complete when first viewed (like TASK-099 where the directory already existed), immediately mark Done with a finalSummary explaining what was found. Don't re-create what already exists.

continual-learning-session-end hook updated to write ONLY to .kiro-memory/learnings.jsonl. Reason: global file (~/.kiro/learnings.jsonl) is outside workspace and triggers Windows permission prompts (Add-Content requires elevation). Local JSONL file is inside workspace and writes freely.

When a hook's runCommand writes to a path outside the workspace (e.g. $env:USERPROFILE\.kiro\), Windows may require elevated permissions. Solution: scope all automated writes to workspace-local paths only, and update the hook prompt to explicitly forbid global file writes.

HomeHeroSection background prop must match the nested object shape in home.json exactly: { glitter: string; dashlines: string; dots: string }. The spec said to handle backgrounds 'internally' but the content JSON already has the paths — pass the whole background object as a prop rather than hardcoding paths inside the component.

Every extracted section component in this project gets id? and className? props even when the spec omits them. id preserves existing DOM IDs used by Playwright tests. className allows one-off overrides at the call site without touching the component.

Barrel export pattern for this project: always export both the named component AND its Props type. e.g. export { HomeHeroSection } from './HomeHeroSection'; export type { HomeHeroSectionProps } from './HomeHeroSection'; — keeps consumers from having to reach into the file directly.

When consolidating multiple section imports from the same barrel into one line (e.g. CTASection, HomeHeroSection, SupportMissionSection, WhySection all from @/components/sections), do the import consolidation and the JSX replacement in the same strReplace pass — fewer round trips.

After extracting a hero/section component, check whether Image, Link, Container, FadeIn, Button are still used elsewhere in the page before removing those imports. In home page all were still needed for feature, curriculum, collaborators, news sections — no cleanup required.

Task specs can overcount affected pages. TASK-103 listed 6 pages for PageIntroSection but only 4 had standalone extractable intro sections. Admissions had no intro section at all; Careers had its intro nested inside a shared wrapper. Always read actual page files before trusting the task's page list.

Nested intro sections (like Careers where intro div is inside a Section+Container+FadeIn wrapper shared with why_join and openings) cannot be extracted to PageIntroSection without restructuring the parent. Correct call: skip and document in finalSummary rather than force-extract.

For tasks that are already complete when first viewed (TASK-099: directory and barrel already existed), immediately mark Done with a descriptive finalSummary. Don't re-create what already exists — check filesystem state first.

home.json curriculum.subjects is string[] not { icon, label }[] — the spec's proposed props shape was wrong. Always read the actual content JSON before writing the component's props interface. The icon is sourced from subjectIconList by index position, not from the JSON.

subjectIconList is a ReactNode[] ordered to match the home page subject list sequence. Components that use it should index by position (subjects.map((s, idx) => subjectIconList[idx])). It lives in src/components/ui/icons/SubjectIcons.tsx and is re-exported from src/components/ui/index.ts.

After extracting a section that was the sole consumer of a module-level constant (e.g. SUBJECT_ICONS = subjectIconList), remove both the constant declaration and its import from the page file. Leaving dead constants/imports is noise and will eventually cause lint errors.

Decorative images that are part of a section's layout (e.g. dotted.png background in HomeCurriculumSection) belong inside the component, not passed as props. Only content-driven images (hero backgrounds, team photos) need to be prop-driven from the JSON.

Import consolidation and JSX replacement can be done in parallel strReplace calls in the same turn — no need to wait for the import update before replacing the JSX block. Both are independent edits to different parts of the file.

When a section component wraps Section+Container internally, the id prop on the component maps to the Section's id prop, not to an inner div. This preserves Playwright test selectors that target the section element by id.

AutoScrollCarouselImage is exported from src/components/ui/AutoScrollCarousel.tsx but NOT re-exported from src/components/ui/index.ts. Importing it from '@/components/ui' fails. Always grep the ui barrel index before assuming a type is re-exported — import directly from the source file when it's missing from the barrel.

When a section component wraps a raw <section> tag (not the Section layout primitive), preserve the raw tag in the extracted component. AboutHeroCarouselSection uses <section> not <Section> because the original JSX used a raw tag — don't silently upgrade to the layout primitive.

After extracting a section that was the sole consumer of a ui import (e.g. AutoScrollCarousel in about/page.tsx), remove that import from the page file immediately in the same pass. Grep the page file for the symbol first to confirm it's no longer referenced before removing.

carousel images in about.json live at sections.hero.carousel (not a top-level carousel key). The task spec said 'add carousel.images array' but the data was already there under hero.carousel. Always check the actual JSON structure before assuming content needs to be added.

Small wrapper components (9 lines of JSX) are still worth extracting — they encapsulate the background colour, padding, and fadeColor defaults so callers don't need to know those details. The value is in hiding layout decisions, not just line count reduction.

note: When a page builds derived data (e.g. missionTabs) from content JSON before passing to a component, move that mapping logic inside the new section component — keeps the page file clean and the component self-contained.
TabsPanel does not require 'use client' on the wrapper section component — TabsPanel itself is already a client component, so the section can remain a server component.

When extracting a section that uses a local type (e.g. MissionTab), delete the type from the page file after extraction — the type either moves into the component or is inlined as an anonymous shape in the props interface.

insight: Context-transfer sessions: when resuming from a summary, read the listed files immediately before doing anything else. The summary describes intent but the actual file state may differ — e.g. imports already partially cleaned up, or a type already removed by a prior partial run.

AboutMissionVisionSection: tab mapping (tab.text →

{tab.text}

) belongs inside the component, not in the page. The page passes raw JSON shape { id, label, text }[] and the component owns the JSX transformation. This keeps the page file as a pure data-passing layer.

When a page has both a local type (MissionTab) and a derived const (missionTabs) that only exist to feed one section, both can be deleted in a single strReplace that also removes the destructured key from the sections spread. Three cleanup steps collapse into one edit.

Task spec for TASK-107 said the component should be a Client Component ('use client') because tabs require interactive state. This was wrong — TabsPanel is already 'use client', so the section wrapper is a server component. Always check whether the interactive child already carries the directive before adding it to the parent.

Parallel strReplace pattern for multi-edit extractions: (1) update import line, (2) remove local type, (3) remove derived const from destructure, (4) replace inline JSX block — all four are independent edits and can be issued simultaneously rather than sequentially.

After every extraction, the page file should have: fewer imports (removed the ui component now wrapped by the section), fewer types (removed local type aliases), fewer consts (removed derived data), and fewer JSX lines (replaced block with single component call). If any of these four counts didn't decrease, something was missed.

Import consolidation gone wrong: when replacing one import line with a new one that includes an additional symbol, do NOT also delete the original line in a separate strReplace — the two edits can collide and wipe the entire import block. Do it in a single strReplace that replaces the old line with the new combined line.

TASK-108: accidentally removed the entire sections import block while trying to clean up the FadeIn/TabsPanel import. The fix was to restore it immediately with a corrected strReplace. Root cause: two sequential strReplace calls on adjacent lines — the second matched a now-modified context. Always read the file state after each strReplace before issuing the next one on the same region.

After any strReplace that touches imports, do a readCode pass before proceeding to the JSX replacement. Import region edits are the most fragile — a missed context line causes the wrong block to be replaced. The extra read costs one step but prevents multi-step recovery.

When a local type in a page file (e.g. TeamMember) is used only for a cast inside a .map(), it can be deleted entirely after extraction — the component owns the type and re-exports it from the barrel. The page file needs no cast at all since it passes the raw JSON array directly.

For sections with optional sub-elements (boardImage + boardCaption), model them as separate optional props rather than a nested object. This matches the flat shape of the content JSON and avoids needing to construct an intermediate object at the call site.

Webflow HTML verification for team section confirmed: image wrappers have class 'hide' — they are intentionally hidden in the live site. The Next.js implementation correctly mirrors this with className='hidden' on the image wrapper div. Don't remove the hidden wrapper — it's a deliberate design decision preserved from Webflow.

When a page still has a hidden/placeholder section (e.g. the office Section with className='hidden'), its layout primitives (Section, Container, FadeIn) must stay in the import list even after extracting other sections. Always scan the full page for remaining usages before removing any import.

When a task requires adding a key to a content JSON file (e.g. banner key to admissions.json), always grep the file first to confirm the key doesn't already exist before adding it. Avoids duplicate keys and JSON parse errors.

For single-image banner sections, the padding/spacing classes (pb-[3rem] pt-[2rem]) belong on the Section wrapper, not hardcoded inside the component. Pass them via className or encode them directly on the Section — either way they stay in the component, not the page.

When the page has a hardcoded image alt that differs from the Webflow source alt, use the Webflow alt — it was written by a human for accessibility and is more descriptive. The page's placeholder alt ('Colorful admissions banner with abstract school-themed shapes') was replaced with the Webflow alt which describes the actual image content.

For small single-purpose sections (1 story point, single Image), all four steps — add JSON key, create component, update barrel, update page — can be done in a single parallel batch: strReplace on JSON + fsWrite component + strReplace barrel + strReplace page import, then a second batch for the JSX replacement. Minimises round trips.

After replacing a section that used next/image, always grep the page for remaining 'Image' usages before removing the import. In admissions/page.tsx, Image was still needed for the requirements section (file.png / pdf.png thumbnails). Removing it would have caused a TS error.

className prop on section components: when the component has its own padding baked in (pb-[3rem] pt-[2rem] on the Section), the className prop should still be accepted and appended — but use `className ?? ''` not a template literal with undefined, to avoid injecting the string 'undefined' into the DOM.
